revision.text:
[[ja:天久宮]][[zh:天久宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ameku-gū | image = File:Ameku-gū Haiden.JPG | location = {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|qid=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan, 3-19-3 Tomari | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 41.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 57.4 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Chenghua era (1465 - 1487) | main hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | other names = | notable for = Ryukyu Eight Shrines | festival = May 15th (lunar calendar: April 5th) | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|qid=Q181966|1=Naha}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.228331|N|127.682628|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|qid=Q11573652|1=Eight Ryukyu Shrines}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} }} [[File:Ameku-gū.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Torii]] [[File:Ameku-gū Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main hall]] '''Ameku-gū''' (天久宮, Ameku-gū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|qid=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ;Kumano Gongen *{{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == Invasion of Ryukyu == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|en|Invasion of Ryukyu|qid=Q1140134|lt=Invasion of Ryukyu|1=Invasion of Ryukyu}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|qid=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|Shō Hō|en|Shō Hō|qid=Q1041365|lt=1625|1=Shō Hō}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|Shō Ken|en|Shō Ken|qid=Q1061074|lt=New|1=Shō Ken}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|qid=Q843010|lt=Shō Shōken|1=Shō Shōken}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021|qq=}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Ryukyu Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|qq=}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021|qq=}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|qid=Q11390939|lt=Asato-Hachimangū‎|1=Hachiman shrine}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596|qq=}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70|qq=}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73|qq=}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73|qq=}}。|group=Note|qq=}} == History == The "Amekuyama Daigongen Engi" in the "Section of Seigen-ji Temple at Amekuyama" of Volume 11 of the ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom'' (1713, Kangxi 52) states that the shrine was founded during the Chenghua era (1465 - 1487). According to legend, an old man named Okina from Mekaru village saw a noble woman descending from the mountain with a dignified monk. They would meet at a spring in a small cave, sometimes disappearing inside. When Okina reported this to the king, the officials confirmed the sighting and built a shrine. The monk revealed that they were manifestations of Kumano Gongen and Benzaiten, the guardian deity of the land. The ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom'' serve as the current shrine tradition, and this founding legend is also introduced in ''Shrines of Okinawa'' (2000). As one of the eight temples of the Shingon sect, Seigen-ji (still adjacent to the shrine) was recognized as one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines under the government system. The government provided salaries and maintenance costs for shrine priests and other personnel. The clothing for rituals was also supplied by the government. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated as an unranked shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. The Okinawa Prefectural Government planned to classify it as a village shrine, but due to economic constraints, it remained unranked. The shrine buildings deteriorated, and the haiden was lost, with the honden collapsing in 1937. However, the shrine's information board states that the buildings were destroyed in the 1944 air raids during the Pacific War. After losing the buildings, the shrine was maintained as an utaki (sacred grove) until the current shrine buildings were constructed in 1972. == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Ameku-gū|qq=}} *[[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] *[[Seigen-ji Temple]] (Bettoji) == References == {{References|qq=}} {{Reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Chuchu Ryotei|title=Ryukyu Shinto Records|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|qq=}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|qq=}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|qq=}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= |qq=}} * == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ameku.html Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office Official Site Ameku-gū] {{commonscat|Ameku-gū|qq=}} {{Okinawan Shrines|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|天久宮|version=94116726|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:%E5%A4%A9%E4%B9%85%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:天久宮]] {{Commons category|Ameku-gū}} [[zh:天久宮]] {{Commons category|Ameku-gū}} [[zh:天久宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11442211}} [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:那覇市の歴史]]
